concepts.title: Short-Run Supply Curve
concepts.description: In the short-run, the supply curve slopes upward to the right. This is because firms are willing to supply more at higher prices and less at lower prices.

concepts.title: Factors Affecting Supply
concepts.description: The supply of a product is influenced by various factors, including the price of the product itself, the prices of inputs, technology, expectations, and government policies.

concepts.title: Law of Increasing Costs
concepts.description: As the output increases in the short-run, the law of increasing costs states that the average and marginal costs will rise. This is because firms face increasing costs of production.

concepts.title: Supply Schedule
concepts.description: The supply schedule is a table that shows the quantity supplied by a firm at different price levels. The mnemonic 'PQ = Supply' can be used to remember this.

concepts.title: Short-Run vs Long-Run Supply
concepts.description: Students often confuse the short-run and long-run supply curves. Remember that the short-run supply curve slopes upward, while the long-run supply curve is horizontal, assuming constant costs.

concepts.title: Types of Supply
concepts.description: There are two types of supply: market supply and individual supply. Market supply is the sum of individual supplies of all firms producing a particular product.

concepts.title: Supply Function
concepts.description: A supply function is a mathematical representation of the relationship between the price of a product and the quantity supplied. It is usually represented as Qs = f(P).

concepts.title: Price elasticity of supply
concepts.description: The price elasticity of supply measures the responsiveness of the quantity supplied to changes in the price of a product. It is calculated as (% change in quantity supplied) / (% change in price).
